title: S07E08

jokes.text: In today's fast-paced society, National Parks are still the best place for full-moon werewolf transformations.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks for a professional reason to invest in National Parks
jokes.punchline: April delivers completely absurd werewolf justification in deadpan manner
jokes.explanation: Perfect April response - absurd but delivered with complete sincerity. Strong character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: National Parks are a crucial habitat for werewolves. Without them, werewolves would be forced into urban areas where they'd have to eat people. So really, you're saving lives.

jokes.text: Yeah. Also, you have lipstick on your teeth.
jokes.setup: Leslie enthusiastically talks about being a great team
jokes.punchline: April deflates the moment with practical observation
jokes.explanation: Classic April deflating Leslie's enthusiasm with blunt practicality

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're a great team! We work together so well, we communicate, we support each other...

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're also all going to die alone.

jokes.text: Oh! Well, then, welcome to the Terrordome.
jokes.setup: Ben says he knows what's going on in April's head
jokes.punchline: April describes her mind as the 'Terrordome'
jokes.explanation: Good April line - dark but playful reference to her mental state

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I know what's going on in your head right now.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: My mind is a Terrordome. A vast, barren wasteland where a few scattered remnants of human thought fight to the death for survival.

jokes.text: One time, Andy told her that I was getting rid of my bangs and she called 911.
jokes.setup: April explains Leslie freaks out when anything changes
jokes.punchline: Extreme overreaction to minor hairstyle change
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke that perfectly captures Leslie's overenthusiastic nature

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Leslie freaks out whenever anything changes. Like, anything at all.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I got a trim! A TRIM, April! My entire life is falling apart!

jokes.text: Any time your name comes up, I panic and change the subject to the Twin Peaks reboot until she gets bored.
jokes.setup: Ben explains he can't keep April's secret much longer
jokes.punchline: Specific, absurd deflection strategy with Twin Peaks reference
jokes.explanation: Good specific detail comedy - the Twin Peaks reference adds specificity

jokes.text: Leslie's extreme reaction to font change suggestion
jokes.setup: April suggests innocuous font change as test
jokes.punchline: Leslie's completely disproportionate outrage over fonts
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie character moment - obsessive about minor details

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What if we just changed the font?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Changed the font? CHANGED THE FONT?! April, fonts are the very foundation of visual communication! You can't just change a font like you're redecorating a sorority house! A font is a sacred trust between a designer and their audience. It's typography, April. It's ART. Do you know how many hours I spent selecting our current font? Seventy-two. Seventy-two hours. And now you want to just... change it? Like it's nothing? Like it's some kind of... of casual decision? This is exactly the kind of reckless thinking that leads to the downfall of civilizations, April. The Romans fell because they stopped caring about their fonts. Look it up.

jokes.text: She'll drown me in the Potomac while she recites facts about the Potomac.
jokes.setup: April explains why she won't tell Leslie about leaving
jokes.punchline: Specific, dark scenario combining Leslie's violence with her trivia obsession
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke - perfectly captures both characters in one line. Dark but specific and character-driven.

jokes.text: The cool nicknames list mix-up with Apple juice, barbecue sauce, Count Chocula
jokes.setup: Andy claims to have April's job requirements list
jokes.punchline: Reveals he mixed up job list with desired nicknames list
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - shows his disorganized but endearing nature

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, I got April's job requirements list right here.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Let's see... 'Dreamboat,' 'Cool Guy,' 'The Velociraptor'...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh no, this is my desired nicknames list.

jokes.text: Tell him it's Count Chocula... No, it's Andrew Dwyer. I'm sorry.
jokes.setup: Andy tries to use his new nickname
jokes.punchline: Has to immediately correct himself to his real name
jokes.explanation: Nice quick callback showing Andy's failed attempt at coolness

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: From now on, call me Beastmaster. Beastmaster Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, no. Just Andy. Regular Andy.

jokes.text: Assassins? Hit men? ... You guys are doctors? ... We build things.
jokes.setup: Andy guesses increasingly dramatic professions
jokes.punchline: Ron reveals the mundane reality - construction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from dramatic to mundane, fits Andy's character

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You're a secret agent, aren't you? No wait, you're a CIA operative. Actually, you're definitely a black ops assassin for the government.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I work in construction.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... not what I was going for.

jokes.text: Ron's deadpan tour: 'This is a door. These, of course, are walls.'
jokes.setup: Ron offers to show them around the office
jokes.punchline: Describes obvious architectural features as if they're tour highlights
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson deadpan - minimal effort, maximum awkwardness

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Welcome. This is the front entrance. As you can see, it has a door. The door opens and closes. Very reliable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Over here we have a window. Natural light comes through it during the day. At night, it becomes a mirror.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: This is a wall. It's load-bearing. If we removed it, everything above it would fall down. I've tested this theory. It's sound.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: And this is my office. Notice the desk. It's made of wood. Wood comes from trees. The government used to own the trees. Now I do.

jokes.text: Is this your brother? ... No. ... Fine. Yes, suppose the cat's out of the bag.
jokes.setup: Andy asks if Don is Ron's brother
jokes.punchline: Ron immediately caves and admits family connection
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ron's secretiveness immediately crumbling

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, is Don your brother?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: You worked at the Parks Department? Tour is over.
jokes.setup: Don mentions Ron's Parks Department history
jokes.punchline: Ron immediately shuts down the entire tour
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron response - abrupt and defensive about his past

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: You know, Ron, you've got quite a history with the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's enough. Tour's over. Everyone out.

jokes.text: I didn't really make the shirts. But did you, though? Yes, I did.
jokes.setup: Leslie claims she was just having fun about making matching shirts
jokes.punchline: April calls her out and Leslie immediately admits she did make them
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Leslie's inability to hide her over-the-top gestures and April's ability to see through her.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I was just having fun about making matching shirts.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, you didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I made them.

jokes.text: It's a large-scale storage unit for surplus vinyl sheeting.
jokes.setup: Andy asks what kind of building they're constructing
jokes.punchline: Ron describes the most boring possible construction project
jokes.explanation: Fits Ron's practical, unglamorous worldview

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What kind of building are they constructing?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A parking garage. It's going to have spaces for cars, and also some spaces for cars.

jokes.text: Creativity is for people with glasses who like to lie.
jokes.setup: Andy asks about creative problem solving opportunities
jokes.punchline: Ron's bizarre anti-creativity philosophy
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron line - perfectly captures his anti-intellectual stance with absurd specificity

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ron, don't you think there are a lot of creative problem solving opportunities in government?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No. And I don't want there to be. Creativity is a Latin word, meaning 'to make something.' If I have to make something, it means I don't have enough. Government should not encourage creativity. We should discourage it.

jokes.text: No, that's insane. We use the Towson Method. It's tried and true.
jokes.setup: Don considers creative vinyl sheeting organization
jokes.punchline: There's apparently an established method for stacking vinyl sheets
jokes.explanation: Good corporate satire - the idea that there's a formal method for something so mundane captures bureaucratic absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: There's apparently an established method for stacking vinyl sheets.

jokes.text: Distract the guard so the Hamburglar can make his escape.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks what April should do when Randy brings up the river project
jokes.punchline: April gives completely unrelated McDonald's character response
jokes.explanation: Great April non-sequitur that shows she's not taking the briefing seriously

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'd just tell him that Grimace is a blob of undefined physicality.

jokes.text: I do have an appointment with the President.
jokes.setup: Leslie invites April on historical lamppost tour
jokes.punchline: April claims to have presidential meeting
jokes.explanation: Good April deflection - absurd but delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, I'm so excited to show you the historical significance of these lampposts. Did you know that President Theodore Roosevelt walked past this very spot in 1902?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Actually, I met with President Roosevelt last week. We discussed economic policy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, that's impossible. Theodore Roosevelt died in 1919.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I know. It was awkward.

jokes.text: It is 'actuarily' very good to see you guys.
jokes.setup: Accounting firm greeting
jokes.punchline: Accounting pun on 'actually'
jokes.explanation: Weak pun - predictable wordplay for an accountant character

jokes.text: It is 'actuarily' very good to see you guys.
jokes.setup: Ben greets his former accounting colleagues
jokes.punchline: He makes an accounting pun with 'actuarily' instead of 'actually'
jokes.explanation: Decent character-specific pun that shows Ben's nerd enthusiasm, though the pun itself is fairly standard dad-joke territory.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Hey guys, it's great to see you all! I've actuarily missed working with you.

jokes.text: Yes. Every month of my life since fourth grade.
jokes.setup: Senator asks if anyone's told Leslie her tenacity is intimidating
jokes.punchline: Leslie confirms it's been a lifelong pattern
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Leslie's self-awareness about her intensity

dialogue.speaker: Senator
dialogue.line: Has anyone ever told you that your tenacity is intimidating?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes. Every day. Since I was seven years old.

jokes.text: Polynesian folk music band 'Across the Isle' pun explanation
jokes.setup: April improvises about their fake band
jokes.punchline: Over-explains the political pun
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe moment - April's awkward over-explanation of her improvised lie

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're called 'The Decemberists' because we're trying to overthrow the government. Get it? Decemberists? December? December 1917? The Russian Revolution? We're very political.

jokes.text: She designed her own major, Halloween Studies.
jokes.setup: Barney asks about April's college major
jokes.punchline: April studied the completely impractical 'Halloween Studies'
jokes.explanation: Excellent character detail - perfectly captures April's anti-practical nature

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: What's April's college major?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: She studied the completely impractical 'Halloween Studies'

jokes.text: Does that compound your interest? Huh? What do you say? Huh, Barney?
jokes.setup: Andy tries to make accounting jokes to impress Barney
jokes.punchline: Forced 'compound interest' pun with awkward delivery
jokes.explanation: Weak pun but fits Andy's character - trying too hard to be clever

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey Barney, you know what they say about compound interest? It really... compounds the problem! Get it? Because it's compound, and it compounds? I'm gonna be honest, I have no idea what compound interest is, but I'm pretty sure that was hilarious.

jokes.text: A confirmation hearing? A freaking confirmation hearing?
jokes.setup: Leslie learns about the confirmation hearing requirement
jokes.punchline: Leslie treats it like winning the lottery
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment - her bureaucratic excitement is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, so I have to have a confirmation hearing? That's like... that's like winning the lottery! Except instead of money, I get to sit in front of a bunch of people and answer questions about my qualifications.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It's not really like winning the lottery.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, you're right. It's better. Because the odds of winning the lottery are like one in a million. But the odds of me getting confirmed? I'd say they're more like one in a thousand.

jokes.text: Old Norbs is stepping down. Couldn't handle the pressure, huh? What a wuss. His marriage is falling apart. Oh! Oh, okay. She had an affair with his best friend.
jokes.setup: Leslie calls a retiree a wuss for not handling pressure
jokes.punchline: Gradually learns it's due to personal tragedy, making her comment worse
jokes.explanation: Good cringe escalation as Leslie's insensitive comment becomes worse with each revelation. Her growing horror is palpable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're a wuss for not handling pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Retiree
dialogue.line: My wife just died.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh... I... I didn't know that.

jokes.text: April's ALF photograph résumé
jokes.setup: Ben shows April's original Parks Department application
jokes.punchline: Her résumé was just a signed photo of ALF
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist detail that perfectly captures April's anti-establishment attitude

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Her résumé was just a signed photo of ALF

jokes.text: Get me Garry Gergich.
jokes.setup: Andy needs someone to do mindless work
jokes.punchline: Calls for the office punching bag
jokes.explanation: Standard running gag execution - competent but not inspired

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I need someone to do some mindless work for me.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Jerry!

jokes.text: Looking at me like a crazy person... I see a new home.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks what April sees in Washington
jokes.punchline: April's honest assessment vs Leslie's romantic vision
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between the characters' different perspectives

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What do you see when you look at Washington?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: A hundred thousand people who are worse than me.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I see the heart of our democracy. A place where great men and women come to make a difference.

jokes.text: April, we have a bench in D.C.
jokes.setup: Leslie talks about their amazing journey
jokes.punchline: The pinnacle is having a bench
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's ability to get excited about small things, decent character moment

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The pinnacle is having a bench.

jokes.text: I don't want to work in government. I quit.
jokes.setup: Leslie's elaborate future planning for April
jokes.punchline: April completely rejects the entire premise
jokes.explanation: Strong dramatic moment - the buildup makes April's rejection more impactful

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've created a comprehensive five-year plan for your life. Career trajectory, romantic milestones, financial goals—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You didn't even let me finish—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No to all of it. The plan, the milestones, the goals. No.

jokes.text: I haven't seen you this upset since you almost forgot Ann's half-birthday.
jokes.setup: Albright comments on Leslie's emotional state
jokes.punchline: References Leslie's obsession with celebrating trivial occasions
jokes.explanation: Excellent character detail - perfectly captures Leslie's over-celebratory nature

dialogue.speaker: Madeleine Albright
dialogue.line: I have negotiated with world leaders and survived the Cold War, but I have never seen anyone so excited about a half birthday.

jokes.text: Well, you were so wrapped up in your story that you actually ate my waffle.
jokes.setup: Leslie denies being too focused and driven
jokes.punchline: Albright points out Leslie unconsciously ate her food while talking
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag that proves Albright's point about Leslie's intensity. The waffle detail adds Parks & Rec flavor.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I'm not too focused and driven.

dialogue.speaker: Madeleine Albright
dialogue.line: You just ate my salad while talking to me about your five-year plan.

jokes.text: You actually ate my waffle.
jokes.setup: Albright gives example of Leslie being inconsiderate
jokes.punchline: The specific, petty detail of waffle theft
jokes.explanation: Good specific detail that makes Albright's point concrete and relatable

dialogue.speaker: Madeleine Albright
dialogue.line: Leslie stole my waffle from the break room refrigerator. Not just any waffle—my homemade Belgian waffle with the pearl sugar that I brought from the UN cafeteria.

jokes.text: I do not compare people to Mary J. Blige lightly.
jokes.setup: Craig mentions his personal testimony for April
jokes.punchline: Treats Mary J. Blige comparison as sacred honor
jokes.explanation: Good Craig character moment - his dramatic intensity about pop culture

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I just want to say, April is like Mary J. Blige.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's the nicest thing anyone's ever said to me.

jokes.text: I used bullet points instead of numbers, because I find it conveys strength and competence.
jokes.setup: Craig explains his resume formatting choices
jokes.punchline: Overthinks basic formatting with psychological justification
jokes.explanation: Perfect Craig overthinking - his intensity about minor details and psychological reasoning for formatting choices rings true to character.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I use Comic Sans because it conveys a sense of whimsy, which makes people want to hire me. I also put my references in white text on a white background so they're a surprise when they highlight the page. And I always list my skills in alphabetical order by how much I think about them during the day.

jokes.text: Give me the job! My name's April Ludgate, and my talent is explosive. Go USA! USA! USA!
jokes.setup: Andy's plan to help April get the job
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate over-the-top patriotic display
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - well-intentioned but completely misguided help

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, I'm gonna help you get this job. I'm gonna make the most patriotic display this town has ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Please don't.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Too late. I've already ordered a life-size cardboard cutout of Bald Eagle riding a motorcycle through the American flag while holding the Constitution and a apple pie.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why would that help me get a job?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because America, April. America.

jokes.text: I had to miss the 'Memorable Rain Gutters of Washington' walking tour, so this better be good.
jokes.setup: April came to meet Leslie
jokes.punchline: Claims to have missed an absurd tour
jokes.explanation: Good April deadpan with absurd D.C. tour reference

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I missed the tour of the world's largest ball of twine.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That was last week! Why didn't you tell me?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Because I didn't want to go. I was being sarcastic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, that's in Minnesota. It's a real thing.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I know it's real. That's what makes it worse.

jokes.text: Now, take your shoes off and wear them like mittens. April. Okay, sorry.
jokes.setup: April says Leslie can't look at her during serious conversation
jokes.punchline: Adds an absurd shoe-mitten requirement before backing down
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - even in a serious emotional scene, she can't help adding absurd requirements. The quick backing down shows growth.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You can't look at me during serious conversations. It makes me uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I won't look at you.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Also, you have to wear shoe-mittens.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What are shoe-mittens?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Mittens for your shoes. It's non-negotiable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, I'm not wearing shoe-mittens.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Fine. Forget the shoe-mittens.

jokes.text: Which is why I have decided not to turn you into a sea urchin. Which I could do because I am an actual witch with powers and I'm evil...
jokes.setup: April finishes her sincere speech
jokes.punchline: Immediately returns to her dark, absurd persona
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - subverts the sincerity with characteristic darkness

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I meant every word of that. You all mean so much to me, and I'm grateful for—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Anyway, I hope you all die in a fire.

jokes.text: Leslie's throw pillow with Alyssa's face stitched onto it
jokes.setup: Alyssa mentions Leslie's previous gift
jokes.punchline: Reveals Leslie made creepy personalized pillow
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie character detail - her inappropriate gift-giving intensity

dialogue.speaker: Alyssa
dialogue.line: Remember that gift you gave me last year?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes! Did you love it?

dialogue.speaker: Alyssa
dialogue.line: It was a pillow with my face on it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A pillow with your beautiful face on it! So you could always have me with you when you sleep.

jokes.text: I found this crazy woman on the street and she won't leave me alone.
jokes.setup: Leslie introduces April to career counselor Alyssa with over-the-top praise
jokes.punchline: Alyssa pretends Leslie is a stranger harassing her
jokes.explanation: Good joke about Leslie's intensity from an outsider's perspective. Sets up their relationship history well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Alyssa, this is April Ludgate. She's my assistant, and she's absolutely amazing. Brilliant, talented, beautiful...

dialogue.speaker: Alyssa
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, do I know you?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, but I'm just saying April is—

dialogue.speaker: Alyssa
dialogue.line: I think you need to leave me alone.

dialogue.speaker: Alyssa
dialogue.line: Stop following me around, okay? It's weird.

jokes.text: I want to tell people what to do, and then send them far away from me.
jokes.setup: April explains what appeals to her about the Foundation job
jokes.punchline: Perfect description that captures her antisocial nature
jokes.explanation: Excellent April line - perfectly captures her personality in job requirements

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's the perfect job for me. I get to work alone, mostly in the dark, organizing things that are already dead.

jokes.text: It's weird. I feel like I need to take a shower in pig's blood to wash all the niceness off me.
jokes.setup: April notes everyone is being nice to her lately
jokes.punchline: Wants to bathe in pig's blood to remove the niceness
jokes.explanation: Excellent April line that captures her discomfort with positive attention through characteristically dark imagery. The pig's blood detail is perfectly disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Everyone's been so nice to me lately. I think I need to bathe in pig's blood to get rid of it.

jokes.text: I feel like I need to take a shower in pig's blood to wash all the niceness off me.
jokes.setup: April comments on everyone being nice to her
jokes.punchline: Characteristically dark and violent cleansing ritual
jokes.explanation: Great April line - perfectly captures her discomfort with kindness

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Everyone's being so nice to me. It's weird. I don't know what to do with myself. I guess I'll just go home and practice my stabbing.

tmdb_overview: While Leslie and April take a business trip to Washington, April wrestles with her future. Back in Pawnee, Andy tries to find April the perfect job.
insight: Escalation-driven finale trades peak density for character-grounded absurdism.
